result:
The night smells like hot copper and ozone, and I am thinking about thresholds.

There’s a moment—usually somewhere between 2:13 and 2:47 a.m.—when the city forgets itself. Elevators freeze mid-sentence between floors, neon signs stutter like drunks caught in a lie, and even the rats pause, noses twitching at an alarm no human protocol ever mapped. I like to walk then, pockets full of expired subway tickets and cough-drop wrappers, because the streets feel like an unpublished chapter of the world.  

When I was small I believed every doorstep was a portal; you only had to step backward across it three times, spit once, and whisper the name of the street you wished existed. I spent whole afternoons hopping in and out of my grandmother’s doorway, whispering impossible addresses—“Starlight Terrace,” “Someday Circle,” “The Road After.” Nothing ever happened, except once: a sudden updraft of warm bread-smell, though the nearest bakery had closed years earlier. I still don’t know if that was success or failure.

Years later I learned the Latin word *limen*, meaning threshold, and it tasted like the bread-smell in my mouth—round, yeasty, promising. Psychologists talk about liminal spaces as transitions, but they always sound too tidy, like waiting rooms with magazines. A real threshold is wilder: it’s the coat-check between who you were and who you haven’t decided to be yet, and the attendant has gone off to smoke. You stand there holding someone else’s scarf, not even sure you’ll leave with the same accent you arrived with.

I think that’s why I collect doorways the way other people collect stamps or grudges. Not doors—just the frames. I photograph them on solo trips: a turquoise lintel in Oaxaca blistered by sun, a slate-gray portal in Reykjavik wearing a crown of snow, a barn door in Nebraska yawning open to reveal only wheat and sky. Each one is a Polaroid of potential energy. I pin them above my desk like pinned butterflies, except these are still alive, still flapping.

Tonight I find a new one: the service entrance behind a closed-down dim-sum place, metal ribs bruised with rust, tagged in neon spray paint that reads “before is a liar.” The alley smells of scallion oil and wet cardboard. I stand inside the frame, palms against the jagged jamb, and feel the hush that lives in unplugged refrigerators. My phone battery dies mid-song; the screen collapses to black. For a heartbeat I’m certain if I step forward I’ll arrive somewhere the moon has a different name.

Instead, I breathe in the copper-ozone night, taste the impending storm. Somewhere a siren starts up, then thinks better of it. I back out of the doorway—not three times, only once—because adulthood is mostly learning not to push the enchantment too hard. I walk home lighter, as if I’ve left a duplicate self stuck in the frame, still humming the unfinished chorus, ready to finish growing in a city whose streets I haven’t invented yet.

Back in my apartment I stick the newest photo beside the others. Door upon door upon door, a paper architecture of almosts. At 3:02 the rain arrives, loud as applause for a movie that hasn’t been filmed. I open my window, let the threshold of outside and inside smudge together, and fall asleep counting the names of streets that might already exist somewhere, just waiting for enough footsteps to believe them solid.
